title: Quiz: Practical Deployment Considerations
description: Test your understanding of deploying QML on real quantum hardware

questions.question: What is circuit transpilation?

options.text: Converting abstract circuits to hardware-native gates and topology

questions.explanation: Transpilation converts abstract circuits into executable form by decomposing gates into native operations and routing qubits to respect hardware connectivity.

questions.question: Why do superconducting qubits often require SWAP gates?

options.text: To enable two-qubit gates between non-adjacent qubits

questions.explanation: Superconducting hardware has limited connectivity (qubits can only interact with neighbors). SWAP gates move quantum information to enable gates between non-adjacent qubits.

questions.question: What is an advantage of trapped-ion quantum computers?
questions.explanation: Trapped-ion systems typically have all-to-all connectivity, meaning any qubit can directly interact with any other qubit without SWAP gates.

questions.question: You should always use the maximum number of shots for every circuit in a QML training loop.
questions.explanation: False. Starting with fewer shots in early iterations can save significant cost and time. Precision matters more as optimization converges.

questions.question: What is a key consideration when batching quantum circuits?

options.text: Circuits must use the same number of qubits
Hardware limits on circuits per job and total shots
Batching is not supported on quantum hardware

questions.explanation: Quantum backends typically have limits on circuits per job and total shots per job. Efficient batching respects these limits to minimize job overhead.

questions.question: What does 'hybrid workflow' mean in quantum computing?

options.text: Combining classical preprocessing/postprocessing with quantum execution
Running quantum circuits in the cloud

questions.explanation: Hybrid workflows integrate classical computing (for preprocessing, optimization, postprocessing) with quantum execution, minimizing expensive quantum device time.

questions.question: Why should you validate circuits on a simulator before running on hardware?

options.text: To catch errors and verify expected behavior before spending money/time

questions.explanation: Simulator validation catches bugs, verifies circuit correctness, and establishes baselines before spending resources on hardware that may have long queues and costs.

questions.question: Which are effective cost reduction strategies for QML? (Select all)

options.text: Use SPSA instead of parameter-shift gradients

questions.explanation: SPSA reduces circuit evaluations, batching minimizes job overhead, and simulators avoid hardware costs during debugging. Maximum shots is wasteful for early optimization iterations.
